auditProceduresLibrary.purpose: Comprehensive audit procedures for all 13 FSLI with assertion mapping, detailed procedures, sample testing guidance, working paper templates, and risk indicators

C1_TrialBalanceLeadSchedules.label: Trial Balance & Lead Schedules
C1_TrialBalanceLeadSchedules.description: Reconciliation of trial balance to financial statements, accuracy of lead schedules, mathematical accuracy

existence.definition: Balances included in TB actually exist and are properly recorded
existence.testableStatement: All accounts in TB relate to actual assets, liabilities, and equity at year-end

completeness.definition: All financial statement accounts are included in TB
completeness.testableStatement: No accounts have been omitted from TB; reconciliation captures all GL accounts

accuracy.definition: Amounts are accurately recorded and arithmetically correct
accuracy.testableStatement: TB totals match GL totals; lead schedules tie to TB without error

cutoff.definition: Transactions are recorded in correct period
cutoff.testableStatement: GL cutoff transactions are properly classified between periods

classification.definition: Accounts are properly classified within financial statements
classification.testableStatement: All TB accounts are correctly mapped to financial statement line items

rights_obligations.definition: Entity has rights to assets recorded; obligations are entity's responsibility
rights_obligations.testableStatement: All liability accounts represent actual obligations; asset accounts represent entity rights

procedures.procedure: Recalculate trial balance totals and verify mathematical accuracy
Compare current year TB to prior year TB for unusual changes in account balances
procedures.nature: Trend analysis - calculate variance % and investigate items >15% change
procedures.extent: All material accounts (>5% of performance materiality)

procedures.procedure: Analyze TB structure for duplicate accounts, suspense balances, or unusual account codes
procedures.nature: Analytical review of account naming and structure

procedures.procedure: Reconcile trial balance to general ledger master list
steps: Obtain TB and GL export from ERP system
Create mapping of TB accounts to GL accounts
Compare debit/credit balances and reconcile differences
Investigate reconciling items (reclassifications, consolidation entries)
Obtain and review sign-off on TB reconciliation

procedures.procedure: Reconcile TB totals to financial statements
procedures.nature: Link/tie testing between TB and FS
steps: Identify TB accounts mapping to each FS line item
Sum TB balances for each FS line
Compare to amounts shown in draft FS
Investigate all variances (must be zero after adjustments)
Document bridge schedule showing TB to FS flow

procedures.procedure: Test lead schedule accuracy and completeness
steps: For each material account (>5% PM): obtain lead schedule
Recalculate sub-ledger totals and verify mathematical accuracy
Trace major opening balances to prior year FS/WP
Trace all material posted transactions to source documents
Verify closing balances agree to TB
Review for any reconciling items or unusual entries

procedures.procedure: Review for completeness of adjusting and reclassification entries
steps: Obtain list of all adjusting entries made (manual JEs, consolidation entries)
Trace each entry to supporting documentation (audit schedule, management approval)
Verify each entry has appropriate business justification
Review for proper authorization and accounting treatment
Verify adjustments tie to audit findings and are properly reflected in TB

procedures.control: Segregation of duties in GL posting and account reconciliation
procedures.objective: Prevent unauthorized or inaccurate posting to GL
designTest: Review GL posting responsibilities - confirm separate from invoice/receipt approval
Verify account reconciliation performed by different person than GL posting
operatingEffectivenessTest: Sample 10 GL posting entries and verify authorized by appropriate person
Review reconciliation evidence (sign-off, date, evidence of review)

procedures.control: Automated reconciliation controls in ERP system (if applicable)
procedures.objective: Ensure GL is reconciled to sub-ledgers automatically and exceptions flagged
designTest: Review ERP configuration - confirm reconciliation module set up correctly
Verify exception reports are generated and reviewed
Confirm approval workflow for reconciling items
operatingEffectivenessTest: Observe reconciliation running in system
Review exception reports for a sample month - verify all items reviewed and approved
Test manual override capability - confirm log of any manual adjustments

procedures.control: TB and FS sign-off process with technical review
procedures.objective: Ensure TB and FS reviewed and approved by appropriate management before audit
designTest: Obtain evidence of TB sign-off (document showing approver and date)
Verify FS reviewed by CFO/Finance Director before submission to auditor
operatingEffectivenessTest: Confirm sign-off occurred before audit fieldwork
Review for evidence of technical review and approval (initials, date, comments)

sampleTestingApproach.populationDefinition: All accounts in trial balance with balances > 1% of total assets or liabilities
sampleTestingApproach.samplingMethodology: Stratified sampling based on account balance size - test larger accounts with higher certainty

exceptionHandling.materiality: Any variance >5% of performance materiality requires investigation and adjustment
exceptionHandling.qualitativeFactors: Duplicate accounts, suspense balances, or unusual codes require full explanation
exceptionHandling.furtherAction: Exceptions rolling up >2% in sample indicate possible control weakness; recommend 100% testing for next period

objective: Verify accuracy and completeness of trial balance
Confirm trial balance ties to general ledger and financial statements
Test mathematical accuracy of significant accounts and lead schedules
Evaluate completeness of adjusting entries and reclassifications

scope.populationSize: GL contains 247 accounts; TB includes 215 accounts (accounts with balances)
scope.sampleSize: 35 material accounts tested (>1% of total assets = $XXX,XXX)
scope.samplingMethod: Stratified by account balance - largest 15 tested 100%, remaining 20 selected randomly from remaining population
scope.materiality: Overall materiality: $50,000; Performance materiality: $37,500; Trivial threshold: $2,500

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Obtained trial balance as of 31/12/2024 from finance system
proceduresPerformed.results: TB shows total debits/credits of $X,XXX,XXX; confirmed through system export

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Recalculated TB totals and verified mathematical accuracy
proceduresPerformed.results: TB totals verified - no mathematical errors identified

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Reconciled TB to GL master; traced all accounts in GL to TB
proceduresPerformed.results: 15 GL accounts not in TB identified - all are accounts with zero balances (no impact); confirmed by inquiry

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Tested reconciliation of TB to draft financial statements
proceduresPerformed.results: Successfully traced all FS line items to TB accounts - complete bridge schedule created

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Reviewed all adjusting/reclassification entries; traced to audit schedules
proceduresPerformed.results: 18 adjusting entries identified; all properly supported and approved; entries traced to audit findings

resultsExceptions.details: No errors or exceptions identified in TB reconciliation process

workingPaperTemplate.conclusion: Based on audit procedures performed, we are satisfied that the trial balance is accurate, complete, and properly reconciled to the general ledger and financial statements. All accounts have been tested and reconciled without exception. The TB provides an appropriate basis for the final financial statements.

highRiskCharacteristics: High turnover of accounting staff without documented knowledge transfer
Manual TB created outside of ERP system (prone to error)
History of TB errors in prior years requiring restatement
Weak review and approval process for TB
Large number of adjusting/reclassification entries (>50) suggesting control issues
Significant accounts (>10% of assets) with unusual account codes or names
Inconsistent GL account structure period-to-period
redFlagsDuringFieldwork: Management unable to quickly locate or explain TB reconciliation
TB doesn't tie to financial statements; large unexplained variances
Duplicate accounts or suspense balances (catch-all accounts)
Evidence of post-close entries made after initial TB provided to auditor
GL export doesn't match TB - missing or additional accounts
Evidence of override of automated reconciliation controls
Adjusting entries without supporting documentation
goingConcernImplications: If TB shows significant unusual balances (e.g., large prepaid amounts for services no longer provided)
If adjusting entries relate to write-downs of assets due to impairment
If provision for losses materially impacts profitability shown in TB
fraudRiskConsiderations: Test for phantom accounts created to hide misappropriation
Review for unusual journal entries by CFO or other high-level staff (management override)
Evaluate if TB has been altered after initial submission (timestamp/version control)
Test for cut-off errors that may manipulate period-end results
Review for round-dollar amounts or suspicious patterns in TB balances

D3_RevenueReceivables.label: Revenue & Receivables
D3_RevenueReceivables.description: Revenue recognition, trade receivables, allowance for doubtful debts, AR aging analysis, confirmations
D3_RevenueReceivables.rationale: Revenue is often a fraud risk area (channel stuffing, side agreements); significant estimation in allowance for doubtful debts

existence.definition: Recorded revenues are for transactions that actually occurred; receivables represent valid customer obligations
existence.testableStatement: Revenue transactions are supported by customer orders/shipping documents; AR balances represent valid debts owed

completeness.definition: All revenues earned and receivables due are recorded
completeness.testableStatement: No revenue has been omitted; all customer invoices are included in AR ledger

accuracy.definition: Revenue amounts and AR are correctly stated at transaction date and year-end
accuracy.testableStatement: Invoices accurately reflect price, quantity, and customer details; AR aging is mathematically correct

cutoff.definition: Revenues and receivables are recorded in the correct accounting period
cutoff.testableStatement: Revenue recorded when performance obligation satisfied (IFRS 15); AR includes only amounts due as of year-end

classification.definition: Revenues and receivables are properly classified in FS
classification.testableStatement: Revenue classified by product/service line per FS presentation; AR classified as current vs non-current

rights_obligations.definition: Entity has right to revenue; receivables are entity's enforceable claims
rights_obligations.testableStatement: Revenue relates to goods/services provided by entity; AR represents entity's enforceable claims (no factoring/sale)

procedures.procedure: Revenue trend analysis by product line, customer, and geographic region
procedures.nature: Calculate YoY growth rate for each revenue category; compare to budget/forecast and industry benchmarks

ratios: Revenue growth rate (calculate as: (Current Year Rev - Prior Year Rev) / Prior Year Rev)
Customer concentration (top 10 customers as % of revenue)
Quarterly revenue trend (identify any unusual patterns)
comparisons: Compare to prior year actual results
Compare to industry benchmarks if available

procedures.procedure: Gross profit margin analysis - identify significant changes period-to-period
procedures.nature: Calculate gross margin (%) and analyze by product/customer; identify drivers of margin changes

specifics.calculation: Gross Profit % = (Revenue - COGS) / Revenue * 100; compare current year to prior year
specifics.investigation: Changes >5% require explanation and evidence (e.g., pricing changes, product mix, cost increases)

procedures.procedure: Days Sales Outstanding (DSO) analysis - assess receivables collection trend
procedures.nature: Calculate DSO and compare to credit terms and prior year

specifics.benchmark: Should approximate credit terms offered (e.g., if 30-day terms, DSO should be 30-40 days)
specifics.investigation: DSO materially higher than credit terms suggests possible aging AR or fictitious sales

procedures.procedure: AR aging analysis - evaluate collectibility of outstanding receivables
procedures.nature: Analyze AR by age (current, 30, 60, 90+ days); compare to prior year

specifics.investigation: AR aging >90 days requires assessment of collectibility and potential doubtful debt provision
specifics.redFlag: Significant aging beyond credit terms indicates collection issues

procedures.procedure: Test revenue recognition policy and compliance with IFRS 15 / FRS 102 s23
procedures.extent: Complete review of policy; test application on sample of transactions
steps: Obtain documented revenue recognition policy
Verify policy is consistent with IFRS 15 (performance obligations met)
Identify different revenue streams and performance obligation satisfaction
For each revenue stream: confirm policy clearly defines recognition point
Assess policy for any unusual provisions (e.g., returns, warranties) that could indicate management override
Sample 15 revenue transactions and verify recorded per policy
Test complex revenue arrangements for side agreements or contingent terms
procedures.examplePolicy: Revenue recognized when performance obligation satisfied (goods shipped or service provided). Sales returns allowed within 30 days of receipt.

procedures.procedure: Sample testing of revenue transactions ±10 days around year-end (cutoff testing)
procedures.nature: Transaction testing - invoice to shipping/delivery documentation
procedures.extent: Sample size per risk level (see below)
steps: Identify all revenue recorded in final 10 days of year and first 10 days of next period
Select sample of transactions (stratified: largest items 100%, remainder random)
For each sample item:
- Obtain sales invoice
- Trace to bill of lading or shipping document (confirm goods shipped)
- Verify shipping date matches revenue recognition date
- Confirm customer acceptance documented (for performance obligation satisfaction)
- Review for any unusual terms (extended payment, returns, contingencies)
- Investigate any transactions where shipment date differs from invoice date by >3 days
Document findings - any cutoff errors indicate systematic revenue manipulation risk

procedures.procedure: Test accuracy of invoice amounts against sales orders, price lists, and contracts
procedures.nature: Detailed testing - sample of invoices verified against source documents
procedures.extent: Sample of invoices (see sample testing approach below)
steps: Select sample of 20-30 invoices (stratified by value and customer)
For each sample invoice:
- Obtain supporting sales order and contract/price agreement
- Verify invoice price matches contract/price list
- Verify invoice quantity matches sales order
- Recalculate extended amount and verify arithmetic accuracy
- Verify correct customer identified and billing address
- Review for any manual overrides or adjustments to standard pricing
Document any discrepancies - review with sales/finance management

procedures.procedure: Analytical procedure: Test revenue reasonableness by product line
steps: For each material revenue line (>5% of total revenue):
- Estimate expected revenue based on prior year plus growth rate
- Compare estimated to actual revenue; investigate variances >10%
- Review for unusual transactions that explain variance
- Consider customer feedback, production capacity, market conditions
Example: If prior year Product A revenue was $500k and company reports $600k, verify business justification
Flag significant variances for potential misstatement or fraud risk

procedures.procedure: Test AR aging analysis and evaluate allowance for doubtful debts (ISA 540)
procedures.nature: Analytical and substantive - test allowance adequacy
procedures.extent: 100% of AR balance; sample test of specific items
steps: Obtain AR aged trial balance as of year-end
Recalculate AR aging (bucket by date)
Verify totals agree to AR balance in TB
Test management's allowance for doubtful debts calculation:
- Obtain historical bad debt rate analysis
- Test specific reserves against documentation (correspondence, legal advice)
- For aged AR (90+ days): evaluate evidence of collection, customer communication, or write-off
- Calculate implied allowance rate and compare to prior year; investigate changes
- For customers with large balances in aged categories, obtain confirmation or evidence of subsequent payment
Review allowance adequacy - compare to typical industry standards (usually 1-3% of AR)

procedures.procedure: Perform AR confirmations (positive and/or negative per ISA 505)
procedures.extent: Confirm material AR balances (typically >5% of AR individually)
steps: Obtain customer listing from AR subsidiary ledger
Select sample based on balance and risk
Prepare confirmation request in writing
Send confirmation directly to customer (auditor control)
Follow-up non-responses (minimum 2 follow-up attempts)
Investigate differences between confirm confirmation and our records
For non-responses: perform alternative procedures (examine subsequent cash receipts, review correspondence)

body: As part of our audit of [Client Company] financial statements, we request you confirm the balance of your account with the Company as of [Year-End Date].
According to our records, your balance is: $[Amount]
Please confirm whether this amount is correct as of the balance sheet date. If there are any differences, please indicate the correct amount and provide details of any disputed items.
Please return this confirmation directly to our office at [Auditor Address] by [Response Date].

procedures.procedure: Test for fraud indicators in revenue transactions (ISA 240)

fraudTests.procedure: Identify revenue transactions from related parties or unusual customers; verify business substance
Review sales contracts for contingent terms (e.g., payment contingent on customer approval)
Sample large shipments to customers near year-end; verify evidence of genuine customer demand and acceptance
Obtain list of post-year-end credit memos and sales returns; assess if should have been recorded in current year
Identify any revenue adjustments made by finance director outside normal process

procedures.control: Sales order approval process - orders must be approved by sales manager before fulfillment
designTest: Review documented sales order approval procedure
Verify procedure requires approval by authorized personnel
Confirm credit checks performed for new customers
operatingEffectivenessTest: Sample 15 sales orders from ERP system
Verify each has evidence of approval (sign-off, system flag)
Test for any orders approved by non-authorized staff

procedures.control: Automated invoice generation and review controls
procedures.objective: Ensure invoices are mathematically accurate and customer details are correct
designTest: Review ERP invoice generation configuration
Verify automatic calculations (quantity x price = amount)
Confirm customer master data is locked to prevent changes
operatingEffectivenessTest: Recalculate invoices for sample of transactions
Verify no manual overrides to standard pricing
Test access controls - who can modify customer prices

procedures.control: AR aging report generation and review process
procedures.objective: Ensure AR is properly monitored for collectibility
designTest: Confirm AR aging report generated automatically monthly
Review evidence of management review (sign-off, email acknowledgment)
operatingEffectivenessTest: Examine AR aging reports for several periods
Verify signature of reviewer and date of review
Test for follow-up on aging items (collection calls documented)

procedures.control: Allowance for doubtful debts calculation and review
procedures.objective: Ensure allowance is reasonable and based on objective evidence
designTest: Obtain documented policy for calculating allowance
Verify historical bad debt rate analysis performed
Confirm allowance approved by finance director
operatingEffectivenessTest: Recalculate allowance using documented methodology
Verify supporting documentation for specific reserves
Test that allowance is updated for actual write-offs

sampleTestingApproach.populationDefinition: All revenue transactions recorded in the year; all individual AR balances >$1,000 or 1% of AR total
sampleTestingApproach.samplingMethodology: Stratified sampling for AR confirmations (largest balances 100%, remainder random). For revenue transactions, stratified by timing and value.

exceptionHandling.materialityThreshold: Any individual exception >5% of performance materiality requires adjustment
exceptionHandling.qualitativeFactors: Evidence of fraud indicators (side agreements, management override) requires immediate escalation
exceptionHandling.furtherAction: If exceptions indicate systematic control failure (e.g., >5% of sample has cutoff errors), recommend extended testing or control remediation

objective: Test completeness and accuracy of revenue recognition
Verify cutoff of revenue transactions at year-end
Evaluate adequacy of allowance for doubtful debts
Assess management override risks in revenue area
Test AR existence and accuracy through confirmations

scope.populationSize: Revenue transactions for year: 12,450; AR balance: $2,150,000; AR customer accounts: 385
scope.sampleSize: Revenue cutoff: 35 transactions; AR confirmations: 40; Invoice accuracy: 25
scope.samplingMethod: Stratified by amount and timing for cutoff; largest customers 100% for confirmations; random for invoice accuracy
scope.materiality: Overall materiality: $50,000; Performance materiality: $37,500; Trivial threshold: $2,500; Quantitative threshold for this area: $25,000

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Obtained and reviewed revenue recognition policy per IFRS 15
proceduresPerformed.results: Policy documented in accounting manual; requires revenue recognition when goods shipped and customer accepts delivery. Appears compliant with IFRS 15 - performance obligations satisfied at delivery.

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Performed revenue trend analysis - YoY growth by product line
proceduresPerformed.results: Total revenue increased 12% vs budget of 8% increase; favorable variance explained by increased volume of Product A (new customer wins). No unusual patterns identified.

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Tested revenue cutoff - sampled 35 transactions recorded ±10 days around year-end
proceduresPerformed.results: 35 transactions sampled; 34 properly cut off; 1 invoice recorded 12/31 but shipped on 1/2 (next period) - recommended reversal of $15,000. Auditor adjustment made.

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Tested invoice accuracy - sample of 25 invoices verified to sales orders and price lists
proceduresPerformed.results: 25 invoices tested; all amounts accurately calculated and per approved pricing. No exceptions identified.

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Performed AR confirmations - sent positive confirmations for 40 largest customers representing 75% of AR balance
proceduresPerformed.results: Confirmations sent to 40 customers; 38 responses received (95% response rate); 37 agreed with our records; 1 dispute regarding $8,500 - reviewed supporting documentation and concluded our recording was correct. No further action.

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Tested allowance for doubtful debts - reviewed historical bad debt rates and current aging
proceduresPerformed.results: Historical bad debt rate 1.8% of AR; current allowance $38,700 (1.8% of AR); appears reasonable. Reviewed 5 aged items (90+ days) - none required additional provisions.

resultsExceptions.details: Invoice dated 12/31/24 (amount $15,000) but goods shipped 1/2/25. Revenue should have been recorded in next period.
resultsExceptions.materiality: Represents 0.4% of revenue; 40% of trivial threshold
resultsExceptions.adjustmentsRequired: Reversal of $15,000 revenue and AR with deferred recognition to next period

workingPaperTemplate.conclusion: Based on procedures performed, we are satisfied that revenue has been recognized in accordance with IFRS 15 and cutoff is appropriate (with exception noted above, which has been adjusted). AR confirmations support the existence and accuracy of receivables. The allowance for doubtful debts is adequate based on historical bad debt rates and current aging analysis. We conclude that the revenue and receivables balances are fairly stated.

highRiskCharacteristics: Significant increase in revenue YoY without corresponding increase in operating costs
High revenue concentration in small number of customers (>50% from top 5 customers)
Revenue spike near year-end (>20% in Q4 vs other quarters)
Complex revenue arrangements (multiple performance obligations, contingent terms)
History of revenue adjustments or restatements in prior years
Significant DSO increases suggesting aging AR or collection issues
Large allowance for doubtful debts (>3% of AR) or frequent write-offs
Related party revenue transactions
New revenue streams with limited audit history
redFlagsDuringFieldwork: Customer confirmations returned with exceptions or disputes not previously known
Non-response to AR confirmations despite multiple follow-up attempts
Difficulty obtaining supporting documentation for significant revenue transactions
Evidence of side agreements or contingent terms not reflected in invoices
Significant credit memos issued post year-end for sales recorded at year-end
Unusual transactions recorded by management in revenue area (journal entries outside normal process)
Pressure from management to meet revenue targets
Sales returns or allowances increasing significantly
goingConcernImplications: Rapid revenue growth suggests business sustainability, but verify profitability also improving
Declining DSO or increasing bad debt provisions could indicate collection difficulties and cash flow concerns
Dependence on few large customers - assess concentration risk and customer financial health
If allowance for doubtful debts trending upward, evaluate customer creditworthiness
fraudRiskConsiderations: Highest fraud risk area per ISA 240 - presumed fraud risk in revenue recognition
Test for channel stuffing (pushing products to customers before they have demand)
Evaluate side agreements that modify revenue recognition (e.g., goods shipped but customer has right to return)
Review for bill-and-hold sales (goods held by entity but recorded as revenue)
Test for fictitious sales (verify actual customer exists and transaction is genuine)
Evaluate management override - sales recorded without proper authorization
Test for round-tripping transactions (sale to related party recorded as revenue with no economic substance)
Review for timing manipulation - recording revenue in wrong period to meet targets

D4_InventoryWIP.label: Inventory & Work in Progress
D4_InventoryWIP.description: Inventory valuation, cost allocation, NRV testing, inventory count procedures, obsolescence assessment, WIP valuation
D4_InventoryWIP.rationale: Inventory is material to many entities; valuation involves management judgment (NRV, cost allocation, obsolescence). Significant fraud risk area.

existence.definition: Inventory included in FS physically exists and is owned by the entity at year-end
existence.testableStatement: Inventory items observed during count are genuine; inventory held on consignment not included

completeness.definition: All inventory owned by the entity is recorded; no inventory is omitted from count or valuation
completeness.testableStatement: Count includes all locations (warehouse, retail, transit); no goods excluded

accuracy.definition: Inventory quantities, costs, and values are accurately recorded and calculated
accuracy.testableStatement: Inventory counted and recorded at correct amounts; cost allocation is accurate; NRV testing is performed correctly

cutoff.definition: Inventory received/shipped is recorded in correct period; purchases and sales cutoff is accurate
cutoff.testableStatement: Goods shipped before year-end are not in inventory; goods received but not invoiced are included

classification.definition: Inventory is properly classified in FS (raw materials, WIP, finished goods)
classification.testableStatement: Inventory classified appropriately; obsolete items identified separately

rights_obligations.definition: Entity has title and ownership of inventory; no commitments that impair value
rights_obligations.testableStatement: No consigned inventory included; no inventory pledged as collateral not disclosed

procedures.procedure: Inventory turnover analysis by category - assess for obsolescence indicators
procedures.nature: Trend analysis of inventory levels and movement

ratios: By category: RM, WIP, FG turnover separately
analysis: Calculate current and prior year turnover
Identify items with low turnover (>180 days) as obsolescence risk
Significant decrease in turnover suggests buildup or obsolescence

procedures.procedure: Gross profit margin analysis - identify unusual variances by product line
procedures.nature: Cost of goods sold as % of revenue

specifics.comparison: Compare to prior year and budget
specifics.investigation: Changes >5% require explanation; may indicate inventory valuation issues

procedures.procedure: Inventory balance trend - compare current year to prior year

specifics.analysis: Calculate YoY change in inventory balance; compare to revenue change
specifics.investigation: If inventory grows faster than revenue, assess for obsolescence or overstock

procedures.procedure: Attend physical inventory count (ISA 501)
procedures.timing: Typically at year-end (or near-end date if count performed before year-end)
procedures.extent: 100% attendance at all count locations if practical
steps: Obtain inventory count procedures and schedule
Plan attendance at all inventory locations (or sample if multiple locations)
Observe pre-count procedures (organization, cutoff controls, blank tag inventory)
Test count accuracy by observing several items being counted and verified
Record test counts on independent count sheets for later reconciliation
Observe for proper segregation of goods (e.g., slow-moving, obsolete)
Verify physical inventory matches descriptions (damaged, obsolete items identified)
Observe cutoff procedures (last receiving/shipping documents recorded)
Obtain copy of final count results and pricing list for audit testing
Document any concerns regarding count accuracy or completeness
Perform post-count procedures - trace test counts to final count sheets

procedures.procedure: Test inventory pricing and cost allocation (ISA 540)
procedures.extent: Sample of inventory items covering >50% of inventory value
steps: Obtain final inventory listing with costs
Select sample of items (stratified by value - largest items 100%, remainder random)
For each sample item:
- Trace unit cost to supplier invoice or standard cost
- For manufactured items, trace cost build-up (materials, labor, overhead)
- Verify standard cost is reasonable vs actual (if standard cost method used)
- Recalculate extended amount (quantity x unit cost)
- Verify cost method is consistently applied (FIFO, weighted average, standard)
Document cost allocation methodology and test samples for accuracy

procedures.procedure: Perform Net Realizable Value (NRV) testing (ISA 540)
procedures.nature: Testing whether inventory value will be recovered
procedures.extent: All slow-moving items, obsolete items, and sample of normal items
steps: Obtain post year-end sales data for several months
For items sold after year-end, compare selling price to cost in inventory
For unsold items (particularly slow-moving):
- Obtain recent quotes or catalog prices
- Estimate selling price less disposal costs
- Compare to inventory cost
- If NRV < cost, calculate required provision
Review management's assessment of obsolete items and slowmoving inventory
For items identified as obsolete, evaluate for write-down (cost > NRV)

procedures.procedure: Evaluate obsolescence and slow-moving inventory
procedures.extent: All items not sold in >180 days
steps: Obtain inventory aging report (by movement date)
Identify items not sold in >180 days as slow-moving/obsolete
For each slow-moving item (sample if large population):
- Inquire regarding business plans (further sale, scrapping, donation)
- Assess likelihood of sale at cost or above
- Obtain evidence of management's obsolescence assessment
- Review for any commitments that impair value (pledged collateral, sale restrictions)
Calculate and test obsolescence provision adequacy

procedures.procedure: Test cut-off of inventory purchases and sales (ISA 501)
procedures.extent: Last and first GRNs/invoices ±5 days around year-end
steps: Obtain GRN log for last 5 days of year and first 5 days of next period
For sample of transactions:
- For purchase cut-off: verify goods received before/at year-end are in inventory; after year-end goods not included
- For sales cut-off: verify shipments before year-end are not in inventory; after year-end not included in sales
- Trace GRNs to purchase invoices and inventory records
- Investigate any timing differences (GRN dated Y/E but invoice next period - may indicate not recorded)

procedures.procedure: Test inventory reconciliation (from count to GL balance)
procedures.extent: 100% reconciliation of count results to GL
steps: Obtain final physical count results and inventory valuation
Trace to inventory GL account - reconcile count to recorded balance
Investigate all adjustments (inventory write-downs, obsolescence provisions)
Verify all adjustments are supported and properly approved
Recalculate total inventory value

procedures.procedure: Test for related party transactions in inventory area
steps: Identify any inventory purchases from/sales to related parties
Test prices charged for reasonableness (compare to market rates)
Evaluate for proper disclosure per IAS 24/FRS 102 s33

procedures.control: Inventory count procedures - documented process with controls
procedures.objective: Ensure inventory counted accurately and completely
designTest: Obtain documented inventory count procedures
Verify procedures include: cutoff controls, blank tags, count teams, supervisory review
operatingEffectivenessTest: Attend inventory count and observe adherence to procedures
Test count accuracy - compare our independent test counts to management's counts
Review supervisory review evidence (sign-off, investigation of variances)

procedures.control: Perpetual inventory system with reconciliation to physical count
procedures.objective: Identify and investigate variances between perpetual records and physical count
designTest: Review ERP perpetual inventory module configuration
Verify system generates variance reports comparing perpetual to count
operatingEffectivenessTest: Review variance reports for count results
Verify management investigated significant variances (>5%)
Test that adjustments were made for material variances

procedures.control: Inventory pricing control - review of cost allocation methodology
procedures.objective: Ensure inventory costs are calculated correctly and consistently
designTest: Obtain documented inventory costing policy
Verify standard costs are reviewed and updated annually
operatingEffectivenessTest: Test standard cost accuracy for sample of items
Verify actual costs tracked and variances investigated
Review for consistent application of cost method

procedures.control: NRV testing process - periodic review of inventory for obsolescence
procedures.objective: Identify and reserve for items whose NRV is below cost
designTest: Obtain documented NRV policy
Verify periodic assessment of slow-moving/obsolete items
operatingEffectivenessTest: Review obsolescence analysis performed by management
Test sample of items for NRV calculation accuracy
Verify write-downs recorded for items with NRV < cost

sampleTestingApproach.populationDefinition: Physical inventory items at year-end; costs and prices in inventory valuation

highRisk.nrvTesting: All slow-moving + sample of 30+ normal items

mediumRisk.nrvTesting: All slow-moving + sample of 20 normal items

lowRisk.nrvTesting: All slow-moving + sample of 10 normal items

sampleTestingApproach.samplingMethodology: Stratified sampling - largest items by value tested at 100%, remainder random selection

exceptionHandling.materiality: Any item with cost error >5% of performance materiality requires adjustment
exceptionHandling.nrv: Any item with NRV < cost by >10% indicates need for provision
exceptionHandling.furtherAction: If >3 exceptions in sample, expand testing to understand nature of errors

objective: Attend physical inventory count and verify count procedures
Test accuracy of inventory quantities and costs
Evaluate adequacy of NRV provisions
Test inventory cutoff at year-end

scope.populationSize: Physical inventory count: 25,450 line items; Inventory balance: $1,250,000
scope.sampleSize: Count observation: 100% attendance; Cost testing: 35 items covering 72% of value; NRV testing: 25+ items
scope.samplingMethod: Stratified by value for cost testing; all slow-moving items for NRV testing
scope.materiality: Overall materiality: $50,000; Performance materiality: $37,500; Trivial threshold: $2,500

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Attended physical inventory count on 31/12/2024 at main warehouse
proceduresPerformed.results: Count procedures followed per documented policy. Count teams properly trained. Cutoff controls in place (last GRN #1450, last shipment #1234). No significant issues observed.

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Performed independent test counts during inventory count observation
proceduresPerformed.results: Performed 25 test counts; 24 agreed with management count; 1 variance of 100 units (2% error) - variance investigated and resolved.

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Tested inventory pricing - sample of 35 items traced to invoices and cost records
proceduresPerformed.results: 35 items tested covering 72% of inventory value; all unit costs verified to source documents; no exceptions identified

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Performed NRV testing - reviewed post year-end sales and assessed obsolete items
proceduresPerformed.results: Reviewed sales for Feb/Mar 2025; all items sold at or above cost. Identified 5 slow-moving items (no sales in >180 days); assessed NRV; all items NRV > cost (no provision required)

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Tested inventory cutoff - reviewed GRNs ±5 days around year-end
proceduresPerformed.results: Reviewed GRNs for final 5 days of Dec and first 5 days of Jan; cutoff appears appropriate; goods received after Y/E not included in inventory

resultsExceptions.details: Minor variance in test count (1 of 25 items, 2% error) resolved through management's count review process

workingPaperTemplate.conclusion: Based on our attendance at physical inventory count, testing of costs and NRV, we are satisfied that inventory is fairly stated at $1,250,000. Inventory has been counted accurately and completely, costs are supported and appropriate, and NRV testing indicates no obsolescence provision is required. Inventory cutoff is appropriate at year-end.

highRiskCharacteristics: Large inventory balance relative to current assets (>50% of current assets)
High inventory turnover volatility YoY
Significant portion of inventory slow-moving or obsolete (>20%)
Complex cost allocation methodology with significant overhead allocation
Use of standard costing with material variances
Inventory held in multiple locations (difficult to count and track)
Manufacturing environment with WIP valuation complexity
Consignment inventory or inventory held at third parties
Recent inventory write-offs or adjustments in prior years
redFlagsDuringFieldwork: Count procedures not followed during observation (unauthorized access, incorrect counts not re-counted)
Significant variances between management count and auditor test counts
Inability to locate physical inventory for sample items tested
Inventory in poor condition or obviously obsolete/damaged
Large number of goods held on consignment without clear segregation
Difficulty obtaining supporting documentation for costs
Management unwilling to adjust obvious obsolete inventory
Cut-off issues (GRNs not matching goods received)
goingConcernImplications: If inventory is disproportionately large relative to revenue/COGS, may indicate overstock and liquidity concerns
High obsolescence and write-downs indicate business challenges
Slow inventory turnover ties up working capital and may impact cash flow
If inventory financed through loans, examine covenant compliance
fraudRiskConsiderations: Phantom inventory - goods counted that don't exist (management fraud)
Inventory double-counting through consignment schemes
Artificial cost allocation to increase inventory value and reduce COGS
Falsified count results - counts not actually performed
Inventory held at remote locations reducing audit access
Related party transactions at inflated prices to increase inventory value
Valuation manipulation through inappropriate NRV treatment

D5_FixedAssetsLeases.label: Fixed Assets & Leases
D5_FixedAssetsLeases.description: Fixed asset additions/disposals, depreciation, impairment testing, lease classification (IFRS 16/FRS 102 s20), ROU assets, lease accounting
D5_FixedAssetsLeases.rationale: Significant balance; involves management judgment (useful lives, residual values, impairment, lease classification); IFRS 16 complexity

existence.definition: Fixed assets recorded exist and are owned by the entity; no phantom assets
existence.testableStatement: Assets observed during procedures; ownership verified through title documents

completeness.definition: All fixed assets are recorded; no omissions
completeness.testableStatement: Fixed asset register agrees to FS disclosure; all acquired assets capitalized

accuracy.definition: Asset costs and accumulated depreciation are accurately recorded
accuracy.testableStatement: Depreciation calculated correctly per policy; costs accurately recorded

cutoff.testableStatement: Additions recorded in period acquired; disposals recorded when disposed

classification.definition: Assets classified correctly (PP&E, leases, intangibles) and by asset type
classification.testableStatement: Capitalized items meet asset recognition criteria; fully depreciated assets appropriately classified

rights_obligations.definition: Entity has legal ownership or valid lease rights
rights_obligations.testableStatement: Title verified for significant assets; lease contracts reviewed for rights

procedures.procedure: Fixed asset roll-forward analysis - compare additions/disposals to budget and business activity
procedures.nature: Analysis of changes in asset balances

analysis: Calculate YoY changes in gross asset values

procedures.procedure: Depreciation expense trend analysis - assess reasonableness

specifics.calculation: Depreciation % = Depreciation Expense / Gross Fixed Assets
specifics.analysis: Compare to prior years; significant changes require investigation

procedures.procedure: Asset impairment indicators - identify assets at risk
procedures.extent: All material assets (>5% of net asset value)

indicators: Assets not used in current operations

procedures.procedure: Test fixed asset additions - sample of capital expenditures verified to invoices and authorization
procedures.nature: Transaction testing - capex to supporting documentation
procedures.extent: Sample of additions (see sample testing approach)
steps: Obtain fixed asset additions listing for the year
Select sample of additions (stratified: largest items 100%, remainder random)
For each sample item:
- Obtain supporting invoice and purchase order
- Verify addition is properly capitalized (meets asset criteria: >$X threshold, >1-year useful life)
- Verify cost includes all components (purchase price, freight, installation)
- Verify proper asset classification
- Verify timing of capitalization (correct period)
- For significant items, verify ownership documentation or title transfer
Document any repairs/maintenance items inappropriately capitalized

procedures.procedure: Test fixed asset disposals - sales proceeds and gains/losses reviewed
procedures.extent: All material disposals (>5% of PM) and sample of smaller items
steps: Obtain fixed asset disposals listing
For each material disposal:
- Obtain bill of sale or disposal documentation
- Verify proceeds recorded accurately
- Recalculate gain/loss (NBV vs proceeds)
- Review for unusual terms or related party transactions
Sample smaller disposals to ensure all are captured

procedures.procedure: Test depreciation calculation and policy compliance (ISA 540)
steps: Obtain depreciation policy - review useful lives and residual values
For each asset class, verify useful lives are reasonable (compare to industry standards)
Recalculate depreciation for sample of assets:
- Formula: (Cost - Residual Value) / Useful Life = Annual Depreciation
- Verify appropriate depreciation method applied (straight-line, declining balance)
Verify consistency with prior year
For fully depreciated assets, verify appropriately handled (removed or disclosed)

procedures.procedure: Test for asset impairment (ISA 540)
steps: Identify assets with impairment indicators (per FRS 102/IFRS standards):
- Market value decline >20% from initial cost
- Obsolescence or technological change
- Idle or underutilized assets
- Changes in business operations
For impaired assets, test management's impairment calculation:
- Value in use calculation: estimate future cash flows and discount
- Fair value less costs to sell: estimate market value and disposal costs
- Compare to NBV; record impairment if fair value < NBV
Review impairment testing methodology for compliance with standards

procedures.procedure: Test lease classification and accounting (IFRS 16/FRS 102 s20)
procedures.nature: Lease contract review and accounting classification
procedures.timing: Planning, interim, and final audit phase
steps: Obtain list of all lease contracts
For each lease, review contract terms and classify as:
- Finance lease: transfers substantially all risks/rewards (IFRS 16: recognized as ROU asset)
- Operating lease: other leases (IFRS 16: recognized as ROU asset with lease liability)
Verify classification per lease accounting standard criteria
For finance/ROU leases, test accounting:
- Initial measurement: lease payments discounted at appropriate rate
- Subsequent measurement: depreciation of ROU asset and interest on liability
- Lease liability reduction through payments
Verify disclosure completeness per IFRS 16/FRS 102

leaseClassificationExample.classification: Finance lease (meets criteria: >75% of asset life)
leaseClassificationExample.accounting: Recognize ROU asset and lease liability; recognize interest and depreciation

procedures.procedure: Test fixed asset register agreement to GL and FS
procedures.nature: Reconciliation of fixed asset subledger to GL and FS
steps: Obtain fixed asset register (detailed listing of all assets)
Reconcile register totals to GL fixed asset accounts
Trace GL balances to FS disclosure
Verify no assets omitted from register
Review for duplicate assets or errors in register

procedures.procedure: Physical observation of significant fixed assets
procedures.nature: Observation of assets during audit fieldwork
steps: Walk manufacturing facility and observe equipment in operation
Verify significant assets exist and are in serviceable condition
Observe for idle or obsolete equipment
Note any assets not observed (potential valuation concern)
Review for any encumbrances (e.g., assets pledged as security)

procedures.control: Fixed asset authorization process - capital expenditures approved before purchase
procedures.objective: Prevent unauthorized or inappropriate asset purchases
designTest: Review capital expenditure approval policy
Verify policy requires approval for items >$X threshold
Confirm approval by appropriate authority (CFO for >$50k, Board for >$100k)
operatingEffectivenessTest: Sample 10 capital expenditures and verify approval evidence
Review for any items approved after purchase (control weakness)

procedures.control: Fixed asset additions and deletions recorded in register by fixed asset team
procedures.objective: Ensure all asset changes captured in register
designTest: Review assignment of responsibilities for asset register maintenance
Verify segregation of duties (approval vs. recording)
operatingEffectivenessTest: Test reconciliation of capital expenditure invoices to asset register additions
Verify monthly/quarterly reconciliation of register to GL

procedures.control: Depreciation calculation automated in accounting system
procedures.objective: Ensure depreciation calculated accurately and consistently
designTest: Review system configuration for depreciation calculation
Verify useful lives and residual values configured per policy
operatingEffectivenessTest: Sample calculation: recalculate depreciation for several assets and verify system accuracy
Review for manual overrides or adjustments

procedures.objective: Identify and record impairment of asset values
designTest: Obtain evidence of annual impairment review (e.g., management memo documenting review)
Verify process includes inquiry regarding obsolete/idle assets
operatingEffectivenessTest: Review impairment analysis for completeness
Verify any impairments identified were properly recorded

sampleTestingApproach.populationDefinition: All fixed asset additions during the year; all assets with NBV >$10,000

sampleSizeGuidance.highRisk: 30-40 additions covering 80% of capex; 25+ assets for impairment testing
sampleSizeGuidance.mediumRisk: 20-25 additions covering 70% of capex; 15-20 assets for impairment testing
sampleSizeGuidance.lowRisk: 10-15 additions covering 60% of capex; 10 assets for impairment testing

sampleTestingApproach.samplingMethodology: Stratified sampling - largest additions tested 100%, remainder random

exceptionHandling.materiality: Any addition >5% of PM not properly capitalized/supported requires adjustment
exceptionHandling.impairment: Any asset with indicators requiring impairment review
exceptionHandling.furtherAction: Systematic control failures (e.g., >20% of additions lack approval) indicate need for extended testing

objective: Test accuracy and authorization of fixed asset additions
Verify depreciation calculation and useful life assumptions
Assess adequacy of asset impairment provisions
Test lease classification and accounting per IFRS 16

scope.populationSize: Capital expenditures: $850,000 (28 additions); Fixed assets: $3.2M gross
scope.sampleSize: Additions: 25 items covering 78% of capex; Assets: 20 items for impairment assessment
scope.samplingMethod: Stratified by amount; largest items tested at 100%
scope.materiality: Overall materiality: $50,000; Performance materiality: $37,500; Asset threshold: >$25,000

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Tested fixed asset additions - sample of 25 items traced to invoices and approval documentation
proceduresPerformed.results: 25 of 28 additions tested (78% of capex); all properly authorized and capitalized; 1 item questioned (see exceptions)

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Tested depreciation calculations for all asset classes
proceduresPerformed.results: Useful lives reviewed and appear reasonable per industry standards. Recalculated depreciation for sample of 15 assets - all calculations accurate.

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Performed impairment review - assessed 20 significant assets for impairment indicators
proceduresPerformed.results: No assets identified as impaired. Equipment in use and showing no obsolescence indicators.

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Reviewed lease accounting - tested 3 equipment leases for IFRS 16 compliance
proceduresPerformed.results: All leases properly classified as finance leases; ROU assets and liabilities properly recognized per IFRS 16

resultsExceptions.details: Lease equipment addition ($35,000) recorded as operating lease expense but should have been capitalized as ROU asset per IFRS 16
resultsExceptions.materiality: Represents $35,000; exceeds trivial threshold; 93% of performance materiality
resultsExceptions.adjustmentsRequired: Record ROU asset and corresponding lease liability; adjust depreciation and interest expense

workingPaperTemplate.conclusion: Based on our testing, fixed assets are largely accurately stated. One exception identified regarding lease classification (noted above, which has been adjusted). Depreciation methodology is appropriate. We conclude fixed assets and leases are fairly stated in accordance with FRS 102 and IFRS 16.

highRiskCharacteristics: Significant capital expenditures without clear business rationale
Weak controls over asset authorization and capitalization
Material fully depreciated assets still in use (potential valuation issue)
Frequent asset disposals at significant losses (potential fraud or valuation error)
Complex lease arrangements not clearly documented
Assets pledged as collateral with significant debt covenants
Significant software development costs capitalized
Asset values not reviewed for impairment in prior years
redFlagsDuringFieldwork: Inability to locate physical assets recorded in register
Assets in poor condition or obviously obsolete
Large capex items with no supporting documentation
Capitalization of items that should be expensed (repairs, maintenance)
Depreciation policy inconsistent with asset types (e.g., 50-year lives for technology)
Lease agreements without clear terms or present value calculations
Assets recorded for non-existent leases
goingConcernImplications: Significant asset impairments indicate business challenges
Heavy debt secured by fixed assets - assess covenant compliance and refinancing risk
Large idle or underutilized assets suggest excess capacity and business concern
fraudRiskConsiderations: Phantom assets - capitalize non-existent equipment
Inappropriate capitalization of expenses to inflate asset values and reduce expense ratios
Asset disposals to related parties at below-market values
Lease accounting manipulation to avoid recording liabilities
Removal of assets from register without recording disposals

D6_PayablesAccruals.label: Payables & Accruals
D6_PayablesAccruals.description: Trade payables, accrued expenses, cut-off testing, payables reconciliation, creditor confirmations

existence.definition: Recorded payables represent valid obligations of the entity
existence.testableStatement: AP balances represent genuine vendor invoices; accruals relate to actual services/goods received

completeness.definition: All payables and accruals are recorded
completeness.testableStatement: No invoices received but not recorded; all accruals identified and included

accuracy.definition: Payable amounts are accurately recorded at correct values
accuracy.testableStatement: Invoice amounts match vendor statements; accrual amounts calculated correctly

cutoff.definition: Payables are recorded in correct period
cutoff.testableStatement: Invoices received by year-end recorded in year; invoices received after year-end not included

classification.testableStatement: Trade payables vs accruals properly distinguished; current vs non-current classified

rights_obligations.definition: Payables represent actual obligations of entity
rights_obligations.testableStatement: Invoices relate to purchases of goods/services for entity benefit

procedures.procedure: Payables trend analysis - Days Payable Outstanding (DPO) assessment
procedures.nature: Trend analysis of AP balances and payment patterns
procedures.extent: All major vendors and AP balance

specifics.calculation: DPO = (Average AP / COGS or Purchases) * 365 days
specifics.analysis: Compare to payment terms offered; DPO significantly higher than terms suggests payment delays or fraud

procedures.procedure: Accrual analysis - reasonableness of accrued expenses

specifics.analysis: Compare accruals to prior year; significant changes require explanation
specifics.investigation: Verify accruals relate to actual obligations (e.g., accrued bonuses to bonus formula)

procedures.procedure: Test AP aging and reconcile to GL
steps: Obtain AP aged trial balance at year-end
Reconcile to GL AP account
Review aging buckets for unusual items
Investigate aged items (>90 days) for collectibility issues

procedures.procedure: Test payables cut-off - GRN and invoice matching ±5 days year-end
steps: Obtain GRN log for final 5 days of year and first 5 days of next period
For each GRN, verify matching invoice and confirm recorded in correct period
Identify any invoices recorded without matching GRN (accruals)
Test that goods received after year-end are not recorded as purchases

procedures.procedure: Sample testing of AP invoices - verify to POs and GRNs
procedures.extent: Sample of invoices (see sample testing approach)
steps: Select sample of AP invoices (stratified by vendor and amount)
For each sample item, trace to supporting PO and GRN
Verify quantity, price, and amount match across documents
Verify proper coding to expense accounts

procedures.procedure: Test accrued expenses for appropriateness
steps: Obtain list of accrued expenses
For each accrual >$5,000:
- Obtain supporting documentation (quotation, contract, time records)
- Verify amount is reasonable estimate of obligation
- Review for subsequent payment to confirm accrual was valid
Assess completeness by identifying any obligations not accrued

procedures.procedure: Payables confirmations (optional per ISA 505 but recommended)
steps: Select sample of vendors (stratified by amount)
Send confirmation to vendor requesting statement reconciliation
Follow-up non-responses
Investigate exceptions (differences between our records and vendor statements)

procedures.procedure: Test for unrecorded liabilities
steps: Review post-year-end cash disbursements for items relating to year-end obligations
Identify any vendor invoices received in January for goods/services received in December
Review management's unrecorded liability checklist
Inquire of management regarding any known unrecorded obligations
Review board minutes for any commitments or liabilities mentioned

procedures.control: Three-way matching of PO, GRN, and invoice before payment
procedures.objective: Prevent payment for unauthorized, incorrect, or duplicate invoices
designTest: Review documented three-way matching procedure
Verify system enforces matching requirements before payment processing
operatingEffectivenessTest: Sample 10 paid invoices and verify evidence of three-way matching
Test for any invoices paid without full matching (control override)

procedures.control: Segregation of duties - approval, receipt, and payment distinct
procedures.objective: Prevent fraud through unauthorized payments or goods receipt
designTest: Review assignment of responsibilities - PO approver, GRN recorder, payment approver
Verify distinct individuals perform each function
operatingEffectivenessTest: Review payment evidence - verify different signatories for PO, GRN, and payment
Test for evidence of inappropriate single-person authorization

sampleTestingApproach.populationDefinition: All payables recorded during year; all vendor invoices; all accrued expenses

exceptionHandling.materiality: Any exception >5% of PM requires investigation and adjustment
exceptionHandling.cutoff: Any invoice with timing exception must be reviewed for proper recording

objective: Test accuracy and completeness of accounts payable
Verify proper cut-off of purchases at year-end
Evaluate reasonableness of accrued expenses
Test for unrecorded liabilities

scope.samplingMethod: Stratified by vendor and invoice amount

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Reconciled AP aging to GL - verified completeness of AP recording
proceduresPerformed.results: AP aged trial balance totals $450,000; reconciles to GL. No unrecorded invoices identified.

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Tested payables cut-off - reviewed GRNs and invoices ±5 days year-end
proceduresPerformed.results: Reviewed 12 GRNs in final 5 days of December and 8 in first 5 days of January; all properly recorded in correct period. Cut-off appears appropriate.

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Sample tested 25 AP invoices - verified to PO and GRN
proceduresPerformed.results: 25 invoices tested; all properly matched to POs and GRNs; amounts accurate. No exceptions.

proceduresPerformed.procedure: Tested accrued expenses - obtained supporting documentation for all accruals >$5,000
proceduresPerformed.results: 6 accruals identified >$5,000; all supported by documentation (bonus accrual $35k, utilities $15k, legal $18k, vacation $10k, other <$5k). All appear reasonable.

workingPaperTemplate.conclusion: Based on our testing, accounts payable and accruals are fairly stated at $535,000 ($450k AP + $85k accruals). Cut-off is appropriate and completeness is verified.

highRiskCharacteristics: Significant increase in AP without corresponding increase in purchases (may indicate unrecorded liabilities)
Large aged payables (>90 days) - may indicate disputes or financial difficulty
Minimal accrued expenses when significant obligations likely exist
Weak three-way matching controls
History of unrecorded liability adjustments in prior years
redFlagsDuringFieldwork: Vendor confirmations with significant exceptions not previously known
Post-year-end invoices for goods/services received in current year
Inability to produce supporting GRN for recorded invoices
Significant unmatched invoices in system
Accruals that don't relate to actual obligations
fraudRiskConsiderations: Fictitious payables to conceal misappropriation of funds
Round-trip transactions with related parties
Suppressed accruals to inflate profitability

D7_LoansCovenants.label: Loans & Covenants
D7_LoansCovenants.description: Loan confirmation, covenant compliance testing, debt disclosure, interest calculations
D7_LoansCovenants.rationale: Going concern risk; fraud risk if covenants breached and concealed; complex calculations

D8_TaxDeferredTax.label: Tax & Deferred Tax
D8_TaxDeferredTax.description: Current tax provision, deferred tax (temporary differences), tax losses, uncertain tax positions
D8_TaxDeferredTax.rationale: Significant accounting estimates; complex calculations; tax authority risks

D9_ProvisionsContingencies.label: Provisions & Contingencies
D9_ProvisionsContingencies.description: Legal provisions, warranty provisions, onerous contracts, contingent liabilities, lawyers' letters
D9_ProvisionsContingencies.rationale: Highly judgmental; significant fraud risk (manipulating estimates to manage earnings); legal complexities

D10_Equity.label: Equity
D10_Equity.description: Share capital, retained earnings, dividend testing, equity movements, shareholder transactions
D10_Equity.rationale: Medium risk; testing of movements and compliance with constitutional documents

D11_CashEquivalents.label: Cash & Equivalents
D11_CashEquivalents.description: Bank confirmations, cash reconciliations, restricted cash, bank covenant compliance
D11_CashEquivalents.rationale: Critical assertion (existence); bank confirmations provide direct evidence; potential fraud risk

D12_JournalEntriesConsolidation.label: Journal Entries & Consolidation
D12_JournalEntriesConsolidation.description: JE testing (especially manual/unusual entries), consolidation elimination entries, intercompany transactions
D12_JournalEntriesConsolidation.rationale: Fraud risk area (ISA 240 presumes fraud in JE); consolidation complexity; management override risk

D13_PostBalanceSheetEvents.label: Post-Balance Sheet Events
D13_PostBalanceSheetEvents.description: Subsequent events testing, adjusting vs non-adjusting events, going concern events, event disclosure
D13_PostBalanceSheetEvents.rationale: Critical for FS completeness; going concern implications; disclosure requirements

D14_RelatedPartyTransactions.label: Related Party Transactions
D14_RelatedPartyTransactions.description: Related party identification, transaction testing, business rationale evaluation, disclosure adequacy per IAS 24/FRS 102 s33
D14_RelatedPartyTransactions.rationale: Significant fraud risk; disclosure requirements; economic substance questions
